_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Maps practice 1
questions.explanation: Both calls use the same key, so the second call replaces its value. One mapping remains, and its current value is 4.

questions.title: Maps practice 2
questions.prompt: Which view gives both keys and values for a loop?
choices: grades.entrySet()
grades.keySet()
grades.values()
grades.size()
questions.explanation: `entrySet()` contains Map.Entry objects, and every entry exposes both sides of the mapping. The other views provide only keys or only values.

questions.title: Trace Maps
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Declare variables with the Map interface and construct a HashMap. Each `put` connects one key to one value. The resulting output is '2\n95'.

questions.title: Maps good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Maps?
choices: Program to `Map` and construct a suitable implementation.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Program to `Map` and construct a suitable implementation. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Maps practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: int score = grades.getOrDefault("ENG101", 0);
int score = grades.get("ENG101");
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: `get` returns null for a missing key, which can cause trouble when Java unboxes it to int. `getOrDefault` makes the absent case explicit.

questions.title: Maps practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Null does not prove a key is absent'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
A map is allowed to store null as a value. If that distinction matters, use `containsKey` to ask whether the key exists rather than guessing from `get`.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A map is allowed to store null as a value. If that distinction matters, use `containsKey` to ask whether the key exists rather than guessing from `get`.

questions.title: Maps practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Maps?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Choose immutable, stable keys when possible.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Choose immutable, stable keys when possible. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Maps practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Maps?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Remember that `put` replaces an equal key.
questions.explanation: Remember that `put` replaces an equal key. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Maps practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Maps?
choices: Use `getOrDefault` for a meaningful fallback.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `getOrDefault` for a meaningful fallback. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Maps practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Maps?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use `entrySet` when processing keys and values together.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `entrySet` when processing keys and values together. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
